Programming

Coding

Software Development

Learning To Code

Productivity

7 min read
Just now

--

Most people don’t fail at coding because they’re bad at it. They fail because they’re learning the wrong way.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A few years ago, I met a developer who was convinced he would never become good at programming.
He had spent months watching tutorials, completed several courses, and filled his YouTube history with coding videos. From the outside, it looked like he was doing everything right.
Yet every time he sat down to build something on his own, he got stuck.
His confidence slowly started to fade. His motivation followed.
Eventually, he began telling himself the same story many beginners tell themselves:
“Maybe coding just isn’t for me.”
Looking back, the problem was surprisingly simple.
He wasn’t failing because he lacked talent. He was failing because most of his learning time was spent consuming information instead of creating things.
I know this because I spent far too much time doing the same thing when I was learning.
Like many beginners, I believed progress came from watching more tutorials, taking more courses, and learning more concepts. Every new resource felt like another step forward.
But after years of professional software development, I’ve learned something important:
Programming isn’t learned by watching. It’s learned by doing.
And if I had to start over today with only one hour a day, I wouldn’t spend it the way most people do.
I’d follow a simple 60-minute system that prioritizes action over consumption.
It isn’t complicated. In fact, that’s exactly why it works.

Why Most Coding Advice Doesn’t Work for Busy People
The internet is full of advice that sounds great on paper.
Code for five hours a day.
Build ten projects every month.
Complete multiple courses simultaneously.
Learn several technologies at once.
The problem is that most people don’t have that kind of time.
They have jobs, classes, responsibilities, families, and countless other commitments competing for their attention.
Most aspiring developers aren’t working with unlimited free time.
They’re working for an hour here and there.
The good news is that you don’t need marathon study sessions to make meaningful progress.
You need focused practice.
Because one hour of deliberate work will almost always beat three hours of distracted learning.

The 60-Minute Learning Framework
If I were starting from scratch today, this is the exact structure I’d follow:
Minutes 0–5 → Warm Up
Minutes 5–40 → Build
Minutes 40–55 → Debug and Learn
Minutes 55–60 → Reflect
Simple.
But don’t mistake simple for ineffective.
This framework works because it forces you to spend most of your time doing the thing that actually develops skill:
Solving problems.

Minutes 0–5: Don’t Learn Anything New
This might sound counterintuitive.
Most people start a study session by opening YouTube, searching for another tutorial, or asking AI what they should learn next.
I wouldn’t.
I’d spend the first five minutes revisiting what I worked on yesterday.
I’d look through my code and ask myself:

• What was I trying to build?

• Where did I get stuck?

• What confused me?

• What worked?
This small habit creates continuity.
Instead of spending the first part of every session trying to remember where you left off, you’re able to jump straight back into the problem.
Professional developers do this all the time.
Before writing new code, they first understand the current state of the project.
You should do the same.

Minutes 5–40: Build Something (Even If It’s Tiny)
This is where progress actually happens.
Not while watching.
Not while reading.
Not while collecting resources you’ll probably never revisit.
While building.
If you’re a beginner, tutorials are fine. The key is to stop treating them like entertainment.
Pause often.
Type everything yourself.
Experiment.
Change values.
Break things on purpose and figure out how to fix them.
If a tutorial shows you how to make a blue button, try making it red.
If it displays three items, make it display ten.
Force yourself to interact with the code instead of following instructions blindly.
If you already understand the basics, challenge yourself to build something without immediately looking for help.
Choose one small task:

• Create a form.

• Build a calculator.

• Fetch data from an API.

• Create a dark-mode toggle.

• Make a simple to-do list.
Notice that none of these are massive projects.
That’s intentional.
Many beginners struggle because they aim too big, too soon.
Small wins make it easier to come back tomorrow. And when you keep showing up consistently, progress starts to compound.

The Part Everyone Tries to Avoid
At some point, you’ll get stuck.
An error appears.
Something refuses to work.
You’ve checked the code three times, and everything looks correct.
But the browser disagrees.
Good.
That’s where learning begins.
One of the biggest misconceptions about programming is that progress happens when everything works perfectly.
In reality, some of your biggest breakthroughs happen when things don’t work at all.
Every bug teaches you something.
Every confusing error message forces you to think differently.
Every obstacle strengthens the problem-solving skills that developers rely on every day.
Getting stuck isn’t evidence that you’re failing.
It’s evidence that you’re doing the work.

How to Use AI Without Letting It Do the Work for You
Modern developers have access to something I wish I’d had when I was starting: AI.
Used correctly, it’s one of the most powerful learning tools available.
Used incorrectly, it can become a shortcut that slows your growth.
Instead of asking:
“Build this project for me.”
Try asking:
“I’m learning JavaScript and understand variables, functions, arrays, and DOM manipulation. Give me five project ideas that will challenge me without being overwhelming.”
That’s a completely different approach.
You’re not asking AI to replace your thinking.
You’re asking it to guide your learning.
And that’s where the real value comes from.
The goal isn’t to make coding effortless.
The goal is to become better at coding.

Minutes 40–55: Now You’re Allowed to Get Help
This is the moment to start searching.
Read the documentation.
Ask AI questions.
Browse developer forums.
Watch a targeted explanation.
Reach out to other developers if necessary.
The difference is that you’ve already spent time trying to solve the problem yourself.
You now have context.
You know what’s confusing you.
You know what you’ve already tried.
And because of that, the answer will make far more sense than it would have thirty minutes earlier.
This is where learning starts to stick.
You’re no longer copying solutions.
You’re comparing your approach with a working one and understanding the gap between them.
That’s where experience comes from.

Minutes 55–60: The Habit Most Learners Ignore
Before ending your session, spend five minutes reflecting.
Ask yourself:

• What confused me today?

• What did I learn?

• What do I want to understand better tomorrow?
Then write the answers down.
It doesn’t have to be elaborate.
A notebook, a notes app, or a simple document is enough.
Over time, you’ll create a record of your progress.
You’ll see patterns in your strengths.
You’ll notice recurring weaknesses.
And on days when you feel like you’re not improving, you’ll have proof that you are.

Why This System Works When Others Fail
Most learners spend the majority of their time consuming information.
Successful developers spend most of their time applying what they learn.
That’s the difference.
This framework forces you to think independently, solve problems, make mistakes, debug issues, and reflect on what you’ve learned.
Those aren’t just coding skills.
They’re professional skills.
And they’re developed through practice, not observation.
Programming isn’t about memorizing syntax.
It’s about learning how to solve problems.
The sooner you focus on that skill, the faster you’ll grow.

The Math That Changed My Perspective
Let’s do some quick math.
One hour a day becomes:

• 7 hours a week

• Around 30 hours a month

• 365 hours a year
That’s 365 hours spent actively improving a valuable skill.
Think about how much progress someone can make with that amount of focused practice.
The developers who quietly move ahead aren’t always the smartest people in the room.
More often than not, they’re simply the most consistent.
While others wait for motivation, they keep showing up.
One hour.
One challenge.
One lesson.
One step forward.
Day after day.

Final Thoughts
If you’re feeling overwhelmed by the endless tutorials, roadmaps, frameworks, and technologies competing for your attention, remember this:
You don’t need another course.
You don’t need another productivity system.
And you probably don’t need another tutorial.
What you need is more time spent building.
Tomorrow, set a timer for 60 minutes.
Review.
Build.
Struggle.
Learn.
Reflect.
Then do it again the next day.
A year from now, you’ll either have 365 hours of real coding experience or you’ll still be searching for the perfect tutorial.
From what I’ve seen, the developers who make the most progress aren’t the ones who know the most theory. They’re the ones who keep showing up and doing the work.

Before You Go…
I’m curious:
How much time do you currently spend learning coding each day?
And what’s the biggest challenge holding you back right now?
Share your answer in the comments. I read every response, and your experience might help another developer who’s facing the same challenge.
If you found this article valuable:
👏 Give it a few claps so more developers can discover it.
💬 Leave a comment and join the conversation.
